questions.question: Which of the following is PRIVATE to each thread (not shared)?
questions.explanation: Each thread has its own stack, registers, and thread-local storage. The heap, code, data, and file descriptors are shared across all threads in a process.

questions.question: In the 1:1 threading model used by Linux pthreads, what does each user thread map to?
questions.explanation: In the 1:1 model, each user-level thread maps directly to one kernel-level thread. The OS kernel scheduler manages thread scheduling.

questions.question: What keyword in C provides thread-local storage?
questions.explanation: The __thread keyword (C11 _Thread_local / thread_local) declares a variable with a separate copy for each thread. Commonly used for errno, random number generators, and per-thread caches.

questions.question: Why is a thread-to-thread context switch cheaper than a process-to-process context switch?
options: The TLB doesn't need flushing since address space is shared
Threads run in user mode only
Thread switches don't save the program counter
questions.explanation: When switching between threads in the same process, the address space stays the same, so the TLB doesn't need flushing and the CPU cache remains partially warm. Process switches require TLB flushes and cause cache pollution.

questions.question: What resources are shared between threads of the same process?
options: Code, data, heap, and open files; each thread has its own stack and registers
questions.explanation: Threads within a process share the address space (code, data, heap), open file descriptors, and signal handlers. Each thread has its own stack, register set, and thread-local storage. This shared state makes thread communication fast but introduces race conditions.
